rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the source: it is titled 'Contribution to Implementation Measures Climate Finance N°3 (IM3)' and discusses that 'Implementation of the EFCC will commence in 2020 and it will be updated every 5 years'. This is an implementation/finance framework measure (enabling climate action) rather than a direct mitigation outcome, matching the provided canonical mechanism 'climate_finance_framework_implementation → climate_action_financing_enablement'. Therefore keep in climate set but reclassify away from 'Mitigation'.
policy_statement: Contribution to Implementation Measures Climate Finance N°3 (IM3).
mechanism_description: The policy will commence implementation of a climate finance framework and update it regularly to support financing and delivery of climate measures.
primary_causal_pathway: Finance governance framework → ↑ capital mobilization/allocative efficiency for low-carbon actions → ↑ deployment of mitigation measures → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: Government establishes and maintains a climate finance framework (rules, processes, priorities, reporting) that improves the ability to plan, mobilize, and channel public/private finance into climate-related projects.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced financing barriers and better capital allocation (e.g., clearer eligibility, risk management, pipelines, transparency) increases investment flows and speeds implementation of low-carbon technologies and programs.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater and faster uptake of mitigation actions reduces economy-wide GHG emissions relative to baseline.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling and scaling investment that accelerates implementation of emissions-reducing measures, leading to lower GHG emissions than would occur without the financing framework.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: A finance framework primarily affects emissions indirectly by enabling the funding and delivery of mitigation measures at scale.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such frameworks can also support adaptation/resilience spending, the mechanism as stated (“support financing and delivery of climate measures”) most broadly—and most commonly—drives climate impact through increased deployment of emissions-reducing actions; the biggest climate-relevant effect across all such policies is enabling mitigation investment.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific framework were explicitly limited to adaptation finance only, classification could shift toward Adaptation; if it mainly targets efficiency improvements in resource use, Resource Efficiency could be argued. Those are policy-specific constraints not inherent to the canonical mechanism as given.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing a climate finance framework shares the same enabling pathway—mobilizing/allocating finance to deliver climate actions—whose dominant aggregate climate effect is typically emissions reduction via scaled mitigation deployment.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether financed actions skew toward mitigation vs adaptation, sectors funded, and the strength of emissions impact must be determined from policy-level evidence and is not assumed here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be Adaptation if the finance framework is explicitly adaptation-only (e.g., disaster risk finance) or if implementation focuses solely on resilience outcomes; could also be treated as cross-cutting enabling, but a single primary category is required, so Mitigation is assigned based on dominant expected climate effect.
key_indicators: “Implementation … will commence in 2020”; “updated every 5 years”; “first revision … in 2021”; “Implementation Measures Climate Finance”
instance_edge_case_notes: “Commence” and “updated every 5 years” could be read as a target/commitment, but there is no quantitative target or standard—primarily a planning/governance schedule for a finance framework.
